steps.title: Compare the scope
steps.content: The student wants to compare Fodor's hypothesis with Carruthers's. The notes say Fodor treated low-level systems such as perception and language as modular, while high-level systems such as reasoning were not modular. Carruthers's massive modularity hypothesis expands the idea to all cognitive systems. The comparison must focus on that difference in scope.

steps.title: Reject weak comparisons
steps.content: Choice B mainly gives chronology, saying Carruthers followed Fodor, but it does not clearly compare the two hypotheses. Choice C is factually wrong because both views include low-level systems as modular. Choice D misstates the disagreement by suggesting Fodor denied innate neural modules, when the notes define modularity itself that way.

steps.title: Choose the direct comparison
steps.content: Choice A compares the hypotheses on the same dimension: Fodorian modularity includes some but not all cognitive systems, while massive modularity is more expansive. That is exactly the contrast the notes establish between the two views. The answer is A.
